id=\"wxn0\" />\u003Cp>Need a one-stop-shop for all the things you to know for hurricane season? Look no further. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>From timing to terminology to tips on how to stay safe, these tropics 101 infographics will help you feel confident and prepared ahead of hurricane season. \u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Life Cycle Of A Storm\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Curious how a cluster of thunderstorms becomes a major hurricane? Read more \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/science/weather-explainers/news/2025-07-16-2025-life-cycle-hurricane-tropical-storm\" rel=\"\">here\u003C/a>. \u003C/li>\u003Cli>The \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2025-05-15-saffir-simpson-hurricane-wind-scale\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">Saffir-Simpson Scale\u003C/a> uses wind to categorize storms, but it's not the end all be all. \u003C/li>\u003Cli>Think you know all about \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/safety/hurricane/news/2025-06-06-what-is-a-potential-tropical-cyclone-hurricane-season\" rel=\"\">potential tropical cyclones\u003C/a>? There have been some recent changes. \u003C/li>\u003Cli>Find out what the term \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/safety/hurricane/news/2025-06-03-2025-hurricane-season-what-is-an-invest\" rel=\"\">invest\u003C/a> is short for.  \u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn1\" />\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn2\" />\u003Ch2>Watches And Warnings\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Knowing the differences between \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/news/2024-09-24-weather-words-hurricane-watches-warnings\" rel=\"\">watches and warnings\u003C/a> could save your life. \u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn3\" />\u003Ch2>The Deadliest Threat\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Wind often gets the most attention, but a recent study shows the true \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2024-05-29-us-hurricane-deaths-tropical-storms-rainfall-flooding-nhc-study\" rel=\"\">deadliest culprit\u003C/a>.\u003C/li>\u003Cli>Test what you know about \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/safety/hurricane/video/storm-surge-separating-myth-from-fact-could-save-your-life\" rel=\"\">storm surge\u003C/a>: is it fact or fiction?\u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn4\" />\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn5\" />\u003Ch2>Be Prepared, Not Scared\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Whether hurricane season just started or there's a storm headed your way, we have you covered with all the \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/safety/hurricane/news/2023-05-23-heres-how-to-get-ready-for-hurricane-season\" rel=\"\">best tips to be prepared\u003C/a>.\u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn6\" />\u003Ch2>The Cone Isn't Everything\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Did you know impacts can sometimes be felt hundreds of miles from the center of the storm? Learn more about how the cone is changing this year \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2026-03-24-hurricane-center-cone-of-uncertainty-change\" rel=\"\">here\u003C/a>. \u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn7\" />\u003Ch2>Hurricane Anatomy 101\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Satellite images of storms can be fascinating, but they can also tell us a lot about a storm's growth and development. Here are the parts of a \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/video/the-anatomy-of-a-hurricane-0\" rel=\"\">hurricane's anatomy\u003C/a> that meteorologists look out for. id=\"wxn0\" />\u003Cp>Need a one-stop-shop for all the things you to know for hurricane season? Look no further. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>From timing to terminology to tips on how to stay safe, these tropics 101 infographics will help you feel confident and prepared ahead of hurricane season. \u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Life Cycle Of A Storm\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Curious how a cluster of thunderstorms becomes a major hurricane? Read more \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/science/weather-explainers/news/2025-07-16-2025-life-cycle-hurricane-tropical-storm\" rel=\"\">here\u003C/a>. \u003C/li>\u003Cli>The \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2025-05-15-saffir-simpson-hurricane-wind-scale\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">Saffir-Simpson Scale\u003C/a> uses wind to categorize storms, but it's not the end all be all. \u003C/li>\u003Cli>Think you know all about \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/safety/hurricane/news/2025-06-06-what-is-a-potential-tropical-cyclone-hurricane-season\" rel=\"\">potential tropical cyclones\u003C/a>? There have been some recent changes. \u003C/li>\u003Cli>Find out what the term \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/safety/hurricane/news/2025-06-03-2025-hurricane-season-what-is-an-invest\" rel=\"\">invest\u003C/a> is short for.  \u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn1\" />\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn2\" />\u003Ch2>Watches And Warnings\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Knowing the differences between \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/news/2024-09-24-weather-words-hurricane-watches-warnings\" rel=\"\">watches and warnings\u003C/a> could save your life. \u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn3\" />\u003Ch2>The Deadliest Threat\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Wind often gets the most attention, but a recent study shows the true \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2024-05-29-us-hurricane-deaths-tropical-storms-rainfall-flooding-nhc-study\" rel=\"\">deadliest culprit\u003C/a>.\u003C/li>\u003Cli>Test what you know about \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/safety/hurricane/video/storm-surge-separating-myth-from-fact-could-save-your-life\" rel=\"\">storm surge\u003C/a>: is it fact or fiction?\u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn4\" />\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn5\" />\u003Ch2>Be Prepared, Not Scared\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Whether hurricane season just started or there's a storm headed your way, we have you covered with all the \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/safety/hurricane/news/2023-05-23-heres-how-to-get-ready-for-hurricane-season\" rel=\"\">best tips to be prepared\u003C/a>.\u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn6\" />\u003Ch2>The Cone Isn't Everything\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Did you know impacts can sometimes be felt hundreds of miles from the center of the storm? Learn more about how the cone is changing this year \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2026-03-24-hurricane-center-cone-of-uncertainty-change\" rel=\"\">here\u003C/a>. \u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn7\" />\u003Ch2>Hurricane Anatomy 101\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Satellite images of storms can be fascinating, but they can also tell us a lot about a storm's growth and development. Here are the parts of a \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/video/the-anatomy-of-a-hurricane-0\" rel=\"\">hurricane's anatomy\u003C/a> that meteorologists look out for. \u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn8\" />\u003Ch2>Tropics Prime Time\u003C/h2>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Although \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2025-05-08-hurricane-season-may-june-early-start\" rel=\"\">hurricane season\u003C/a> is defined as June through November, some storms don't follow the status quo.  \u003C/li>\u003C/ul>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn9\" />\u003Cp>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/bios/caitlin-kaiser\" rel=\"\">\u003Cem>Caitlin Kaiser\u003C/em>\u003C/a>\u003Cem> graduated from the Georgia Institute of Technology with both an undergraduate and graduate degree in Earth and Atmospheric Sciences before starting her career as a digital meteorologist with gold_text:
Whether you live near the coast or have tropical vacation plans, it’s important to refresh your tropics knowledge as this hurricane season shifts into high gear. Check out these infographics of the most important tropical tidbits you should know.

Need a one-stop-shop for all the things you to know for hurricane season? Look no further.

From timing to terminology to tips on how to stay safe, these tropics 101 infographics will help you feel confident and prepared ahead of hurricane season.

Life Cycle Of A Storm

Curious how a cluster of thunderstorms becomes a major hurricane? Read more here .

The Saffir-Simpson Scale uses wind to categorize storms, but it's not the end all be all.

Think you know all about potential tropical cyclones ? There have been some recent changes.

Find out what the term invest is short for.

Watches And Warnings

Knowing the differences between watches and warnings could save your life.

The Deadliest Threat

Wind often gets the most attention, but a recent study shows the true deadliest culprit .

Test what you know about storm surge : is it fact or fiction?

Be Prepared, Not Scared

Whether hurricane season just started or there's a storm headed your way, we have you covered with all the best tips to be prepared .

The Cone Isn't Everything

Did you know impacts can sometimes be felt hundreds of miles from the center of the storm? Learn more about how the cone is changing this year here .

Hurricane Anatomy 101

Satellite images of storms can be fascinating, but they can also tell us a lot about a storm's growth and development. Here are the parts of a hurricane's anatomy that meteorologists look out for.

Tropics Prime Time

Although hurricane season is defined as June through November, some storms don't follow the status quo.

Caitlin Kaiser graduated from the Georgia Institute of Technology with both an undergraduate and graduate degree in Earth and Atmospheric Sciences before starting her career as a digital meteorologist with weather.com.

id=\"wxn0\" />\u003Cp>El Niño is expected to begin by July, but its effects may not be seen until cooler weather arrives later in the year. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>This is partially because the strength of this year’s \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/climate/news/2026-03-12-super-el-nino-noaa-summer-hurricane-season-impacts\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">El Niño won't bloom until this autumn or winter\u003C/a>. But that’s not the only reason. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>The other reason is that the overlap between El Niño and weather in the Lower 48 is the jet stream, which is typically strongest in the cooler months when there is the most amount of contrast in temperatures from north to south across the country. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>El Niño and its heating sends large disturbances away from the equator that can affect the jet stream and other pressure patterns over the U.S.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>The jet stream is one of the biggest drivers of weather during the winter when it drives major winter storm development, big temperature changes and atmospheric river events. By the end of hurricane season, the jet stream can thwart attempts at hurricane landfalls on the East Coast while bringing nicer weather to the U.S.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Without the jet stream, weather is more moderate and often driven by smaller-scale weather systems or patterns like heat domes, sea breezes or the monsoon. Localized pop-up thunderstorm activity, known by meteorologists as convection, often dictates the forecast for many communities during the summer. El Niño just simply doesn't have as big of an impact on smaller features like these as it does on large-scale features. \u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn1\" />\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn2\" />\u003Cp>\u003Cstrong>(MORE: Further beef up your forecast with our detailed, hour-by-hour breakdown for the next 8 days – only available on our \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/subscribe?tpcc=subs-editorial-192hrs\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Premium Pro experience\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>\u003Cstrong>.)\u003C/strong>\u003C/p>\u003Cp>There are some effects of El Niño that can be felt indirectly. Hurricane activity is suppressed by stronger winds aloft in the Caribbean, which can lead to a loss of rainfall in the Southeast. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>The opposite can be true in the Southwest, where additional tropical systems can bring more moisture during the annual Southwest monsoon, but \u003Ca href=\"https://www.google.com/url?q=https://www.climate.gov/news-features/blogs/enso/north-american-monsoon&sa=D&source=docs&ust=1778098803625515&usg=AOvVaw2K2rBUMDPIlM0ZyqHYOSEg\" rel=\"\">some studies suggest\u003C/a> that there could be longer dry periods between moisture surges caused by tropical systems. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>One way we’ll likely be talking about El Niño on the other side of this summer is that regardless of any weather effects it may have, it will directly heat the Pacific Ocean, likely drawing us up the ranks of the warmest summers on record. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>\u003Cstrong>(MORE: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2026-04-10-super-el-nino-hurricane-track-history\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>How A Potential Super El Niño Could Affect Hurricane Tracks\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>\u003Cstrong>)\u003C/strong>\u003C/p>\u003Cp>El Niño, by definition, is the warming of the eastern equatorial Pacific, and if some models are correct, that warming could be by several degrees. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>The previous super El Niño crushed previous global temperature records in 2015 and 2016. Those two years remain among the top 10 warmest years for the planet, \u003Ca href=\"https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/monitoring/monthly-report/global/202513#ten-warmest\" rel=\"\">all of which have occurred since 2015\u003C/a>.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Given last year was the planet's third warmest year, it seems like a slam dunk that new heat records will be set in 2026, possibly again in 2027.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>\u003Cem>Note: Some graphics were modified to zoom in on the United States. Their original forms can be found \u003C/em>\u003Ca href=\"https://www.climate.gov/news-features/featured-images/historically-how-has-el-nino-influenced-summer-temperature-and\" rel=\"\">\u003Cem>here\u003C/em>\u003C/a>\u003Cem>. No other alterations were made. \u003C/em>\u003C/p>\u003Cp>\u003Cem>Jonathan Belles has been a digital meteorologist for weather.com for nearly 10 years. His favorite weather is tropical weather, but also enjoys covering high-impact weather and news stories and winter storms. He's a two-time graduate of Florida State University and a proud graduate of St. Petersburg College. \u003C/em>\u003C/p>\n","flags":{"Facebook":true,"Intranet":false,"TV gold_text:
We expect much bigger impacts on weather later this year, but the summer before an El Niño can feel like a regular summer. Here’s why.  

El Niño is expected to begin by July, but its effects may not be seen until cooler weather arrives later in the year.

This is partially because the strength of this year’s El Niño won't bloom until this autumn or winter . But that’s not the only reason.

The other reason is that the overlap between El Niño and weather in the Lower 48 is the jet stream, which is typically strongest in the cooler months when there is the most amount of contrast in temperatures from north to south across the country.

El Niño and its heating sends large disturbances away from the equator that can affect the jet stream and other pressure patterns over the U.S.

The jet stream is one of the biggest drivers of weather during the winter when it drives major winter storm development, big temperature changes and atmospheric river events. By the end of hurricane season, the jet stream can thwart attempts at hurricane landfalls on the East Coast while bringing nicer weather to the U.S.

Without the jet stream, weather is more moderate and often driven by smaller-scale weather systems or patterns like heat domes, sea breezes or the monsoon. Localized pop-up thunderstorm activity, known by meteorologists as convection, often dictates the forecast for many communities during the summer. El Niño just simply doesn't have as big of an impact on smaller features like these as it does on large-scale features.

(MORE: Further beef up your forecast with our detailed, hour-by-hour breakdown for the next 8 days – only available on our Premium Pro experience .)

There are some effects of El Niño that can be felt indirectly. Hurricane activity is suppressed by stronger winds aloft in the Caribbean, which can lead to a loss of rainfall in the Southeast.

The opposite can be true in the Southwest, where additional tropical systems can bring more moisture during the annual Southwest monsoon, but some studies suggest that there could be longer dry periods between moisture surges caused by tropical systems.

One way we’ll likely be talking about El Niño on the other side of this summer is that regardless of any weather effects it may have, it will directly heat the Pacific Ocean, likely drawing us up the ranks of the warmest summers on record.

(MORE: How A Potential Super El Niño Could Affect Hurricane Tracks )

El Niño, by definition, is the warming of the eastern equatorial Pacific, and if some models are correct, that warming could be by several degrees.

The previous super El Niño crushed previous global temperature records in 2015 and 2016. Those two years remain among the top 10 warmest years for the planet, all of which have occurred since 2015 .

Given last year was the planet's third warmest year, it seems like a slam dunk that new heat records will be set in 2026, possibly again in 2027.

Note: Some graphics were modified to zoom in on the United States. Their original forms can be found here . No other alterations were made.

Jonathan Belles has been a digital meteorologist for weather.com for nearly 10 years. His favorite weather is tropical weather, but also enjoys covering high-impact weather and news stories and winter storms. He's a two-time graduate of Florida State University and a proud graduate of St. Petersburg College.

id=\"wxn0\" />\u003Cp>April was record warm in dozens of Midwest cities, record wet in the western Great Lakes and record dry in parts of the East and Southwest as a spring of extremes continued in 2026. \u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Historic Soaking\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>Six major weather stations had their record wettest April in the upper Midwest. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>In Wisconsin, Green Bay, Madison and Milwaukee each topped their previous record wet Aprils in at least 140 years. Milwaukee not only clobbered its previous wettest April by \u003Ca href=\"https://x.com/WI_ClimateSCO/status/2049238635405287814/photo/3\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">over 2 inches\u003C/a>, it had its seventh wettest single month since 1871, picking up almost 9.5 inches. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Statewide, it was easily the \u003Ca href=\"https://x.com/WI_ClimateSCO/status/2049238635405287814\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">wettest April in the Badger State since 1895\u003C/a>, according to the Wisconsin State Climatology Office. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>(\u003Cstrong>RECAP: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/video/milwaukee-drivers-trapped-storms-flood-streets\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Drivers Trapped\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>\u003Cstrong> \u003C/strong>|\u003Cstrong> \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/sports-recreation/video/milwaukee-brewers-american-family-field-roof-leaks\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Rain Leaked Into Brewers Stadium\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>)\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn1\" />\u003Cp>In Michigan, it was the soggiest April on record in both Flint (6.50 inches) and Houghton Lake (8.08 inches), and among the top 3 wettest in Grand Rapids, Lansing and Muskegon. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Statewide, Michigan easily surpassed 2013 as its \u003Ca href=\"https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/monitoring/climate-at-a-glance/statewide/time-series/20/pcp/1/4/1895-2026?base_prd=true&begbaseyear=1901&endbaseyear=2000\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">wettest April dating to 1895\u003C/a>, according to NOAA. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Rounds of heavy rain falling on heavy snowpack triggered \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/news/2026-04-17-michigan-wisconsin-flooding-record-wet-april-spring\" rel=\"\">record flooding in parts of northern Michigan and northeast Wisconsin\u003C/a>, washing out roads, prompting evacuations and flooding homes. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>It even led to the bizarre sight of \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/trending/video/ice-slams-into-michigan-homes\" rel=\"\">piles of ice floating atop northern Michigan's swollen Black Lake pushed into homes\u003C/a>. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Boise, Idaho, had its wettest April (3.87 inches), topping a record that stood since 1912. \u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Dry Month\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>Meanwhile, it was another dry spring month in the Southeast. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>It was the driest April in 60 years in Wallops Island, Virginia (0.36 inches). Charleston, South Carolina, couldn't manage even a quarter-inch of rain (0.24 inches), and Gainesville, Florida, only measured a tenth of an inch in April, its second driest April. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>It was the third driest April in 132 years in South Carolina, and fifth driest in both North Carolina and Virginia, according to NOAA. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>While some soaking rain arrived in other parts of the South before month's end, this persistent dry weather since winter left much of the South in \u003Ca href=\"https://droughtmonitor.unl.edu/data/png/20260428/20260428_usdm.png\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">severe to exceptional drought\u003C/a>, and led to large wildfires in southern Georgia and Florida, among other areas. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>(\u003Cstrong>RECAP: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/health/breathing/news/2026-04-23-homes-destroyed-as-wildfires-spread-georgia-florida-southeast\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Florida, Georgia Wildfires\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>)\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Las Vegas, Phoenix, Tucson, and Yuma, Arizona, didn't manage any measurable precipitation in April, though rainless Aprils have happened many times before in the Desert Southwest. \u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn2\" />\u003Ch2>Summerlike April\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>Eight states from Missouri to Virginia had their warmest April in 132 years, according to NOAA's calculations. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Four other states — Arkansas, Maryland, North Carolina and Pennsylvania — had their second warmest April. \u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn3\" />\u003Cp>According to the Southeast Regional Climate Center, at least 19 major reporting stations with data since at least the 1960s either tied or set their warmest April in history, denoted by the red dots in the map below. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Among those major cities were Atlanta, Columbus, Indianapolis, Louisville, Nashville and Pittsburgh. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Over two dozen more cities had either their second or third warmest April, including Charlotte, Cincinnati, Phoenix, St. Louis and Raleigh. \u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn4\" />\u003Cp>Several eastern cities had a \u003Ca href=\"https://x.com/NWSEastern/status/2049976578172404143\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">record number of April days with highs at least in the 80s\u003C/a>, including Charlotte (17 days), New York City (5 days) and Washington (11 days), according to the National Weather Service. \u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Severe Split\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>The same persistent pattern that led to the dramatic contrast in precipitation in April also led to a lack of severe weather in parts of the Southeast where April has been a notorious month, particularly recently. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>For the first April in 14 years, there was not a single tornado recorded in Alabama in April 2026. The state had 12 other tornado-less Aprils since 1950, prior to this year. Alabama averaged \u003Ca href=\"https://www.spc.noaa.gov/wcm/permonth_by_state/April.png\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">16 tornadoes each April\u003C/a> so far this century, according to NOAA's Storm Prediction Center. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>There wasn't even a single tornado warning issued in the state for the \u003Ca href=\"https://x.com/GumryWX/status/2049927205371724033\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">first April since 2004\u003C/a>. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Severe weather was even more absent in neighboring Georgia. Only three reports of severe thunderstorms — all on April 3 — were tallied in the state all month, its quietest April for severe weather in 25 years. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>The Plains and Midwest absorbed what the Southeast didn't experience.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Wisconsin — \u003Ca href=\"https://noaa.maps.arcgis.com/apps/dashboards/f5eaf19924af427aac8d173d6328758e#date=20260401,20260428&state=wi\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">27 confirmed tornadoes\u003C/a> — tied its second most of any month since 1950, the only time April has been counted among the state's most tornadic months, according to the state climate office. \u003Ca href=\"https://x.com/JustinTGee/status/2050199625852674530\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">Over 300 severe, tornado and flash flood warnings were issued in the state in April\u003C/a>, the most of any single month on record. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>All previous Badger State monthly tornado and \"severe weather warnings issued\" records were in a summer month prior to April 2026. \u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn5\" />\u003Cp>\u003Cem>Jonathan Erdman is a senior meteorologist at weather.com and has been covering national and international weather since 1996. Extreme and bizarre weather are his favorite topics. id=\"wxn0\" />\u003Cp>April was record warm in dozens of Midwest cities, record wet in the western Great Lakes and record dry in parts of the East and Southwest as a spring of extremes continued in 2026. \u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Historic Soaking\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>Six major weather stations had their record wettest April in the upper Midwest. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>In Wisconsin, Green Bay, Madison and Milwaukee each topped their previous record wet Aprils in at least 140 years. Milwaukee not only clobbered its previous wettest April by \u003Ca href=\"https://x.com/WI_ClimateSCO/status/2049238635405287814/photo/3\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">over 2 inches\u003C/a>, it had its seventh wettest single month since 1871, picking up almost 9.5 inches. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Statewide, it was easily the \u003Ca href=\"https://x.com/WI_ClimateSCO/status/2049238635405287814\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">wettest April in the Badger State since 1895\u003C/a>, according to the Wisconsin State Climatology Office. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>(\u003Cstrong>RECAP: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/video/milwaukee-drivers-trapped-storms-flood-streets\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Drivers Trapped\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>\u003Cstrong> \u003C/strong>|\u003Cstrong> \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/sports-recreation/video/milwaukee-brewers-american-family-field-roof-leaks\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Rain Leaked Into Brewers Stadium\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>)\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn1\" />\u003Cp>In Michigan, it was the soggiest April on record in both Flint (6.50 inches) and Houghton Lake (8.08 inches), and among the top 3 wettest in Grand Rapids, Lansing and Muskegon. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Statewide, Michigan easily surpassed 2013 as its \u003Ca href=\"https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/monitoring/climate-at-a-glance/statewide/time-series/20/pcp/1/4/1895-2026?base_prd=true&begbaseyear=1901&endbaseyear=2000\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">wettest April dating to 1895\u003C/a>, according to NOAA. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Rounds of heavy rain falling on heavy snowpack triggered \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/news/2026-04-17-michigan-wisconsin-flooding-record-wet-april-spring\" rel=\"\">record flooding in parts of northern Michigan and northeast Wisconsin\u003C/a>, washing out roads, prompting evacuations and flooding homes. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>It even led to the bizarre sight of \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/trending/video/ice-slams-into-michigan-homes\" rel=\"\">piles of ice floating atop northern Michigan's swollen Black Lake pushed into homes\u003C/a>. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Boise, Idaho, had its wettest April (3.87 inches), topping a record that stood since 1912. \u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Dry Month\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>Meanwhile, it was another dry spring month in the Southeast. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>It was the driest April in 60 years in Wallops Island, Virginia (0.36 inches). Charleston, South Carolina, couldn't manage even a quarter-inch of rain (0.24 inches), and Gainesville, Florida, only measured a tenth of an inch in April, its second driest April. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>It was the third driest April in 132 years in South Carolina, and fifth driest in both North Carolina and Virginia, according to NOAA. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>While some soaking rain arrived in other parts of the South before month's end, this persistent dry weather since winter left much of the South in \u003Ca href=\"https://droughtmonitor.unl.edu/data/png/20260428/20260428_usdm.png\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">severe to exceptional drought\u003C/a>, and led to large wildfires in southern Georgia and Florida, among other areas. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>(\u003Cstrong>RECAP: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/health/breathing/news/2026-04-23-homes-destroyed-as-wildfires-spread-georgia-florida-southeast\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Florida, Georgia Wildfires\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>)\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Las Vegas, Phoenix, Tucson, and Yuma, Arizona, didn't manage any measurable precipitation in April, though rainless Aprils have happened many times before in the Desert Southwest. \u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn2\" />\u003Ch2>Summerlike April\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>Eight states from Missouri to Virginia had their warmest April in 132 years, according to NOAA's calculations. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Four other states — Arkansas, Maryland, North Carolina and Pennsylvania — had their second warmest April. \u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn3\" />\u003Cp>According to the Southeast Regional Climate Center, at least 19 major reporting stations with data since at least the 1960s either tied or set their warmest April in history, denoted by the red dots in the map below. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Among those major cities were Atlanta, Columbus, Indianapolis, Louisville, Nashville and Pittsburgh. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Over two dozen more cities had either their second or third warmest April, including Charlotte, Cincinnati, Phoenix, St. Louis and Raleigh. \u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn4\" />\u003Cp>Several eastern cities had a \u003Ca href=\"https://x.com/NWSEastern/status/2049976578172404143\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">record number of April days with highs at least in the 80s\u003C/a>, including Charlotte (17 days), New York City (5 days) and Washington (11 days), according to the National Weather Service. \u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Severe Split\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>The same persistent pattern that led to the dramatic contrast in precipitation in April also led to a lack of severe weather in parts of the Southeast where April has been a notorious month, particularly recently. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>For the first April in 14 years, there was not a single tornado recorded in Alabama in April 2026. The state had 12 other tornado-less Aprils since 1950, prior to this year. gold_text:
This has become a theme this spring. Dozens of cities set April records for warmth, precipitation and even a lack of rain. Here's who rewrote their April record books this year. 

April was record warm in dozens of Midwest cities, record wet in the western Great Lakes and record dry in parts of the East and Southwest as a spring of extremes continued in 2026.

Historic Soaking

Six major weather stations had their record wettest April in the upper Midwest.

In Wisconsin, Green Bay, Madison and Milwaukee each topped their previous record wet Aprils in at least 140 years. Milwaukee not only clobbered its previous wettest April by over 2 inches , it had its seventh wettest single month since 1871, picking up almost 9.5 inches.

Statewide, it was easily the wettest April in the Badger State since 1895 , according to the Wisconsin State Climatology Office.

( RECAP: Drivers Trapped | Rain Leaked Into Brewers Stadium )

In Michigan, it was the soggiest April on record in both Flint (6.50 inches) and Houghton Lake (8.08 inches), and among the top 3 wettest in Grand Rapids, Lansing and Muskegon.

Statewide, Michigan easily surpassed 2013 as its wettest April dating to 1895 , according to NOAA.

Rounds of heavy rain falling on heavy snowpack triggered record flooding in parts of northern Michigan and northeast Wisconsin , washing out roads, prompting evacuations and flooding homes.

It even led to the bizarre sight of piles of ice floating atop northern Michigan's swollen Black Lake pushed into homes .

Boise, Idaho, had its wettest April (3.87 inches), topping a record that stood since 1912.

Meanwhile, it was another dry spring month in the Southeast.

It was the driest April in 60 years in Wallops Island, Virginia (0.36 inches). Charleston, South Carolina, couldn't manage even a quarter-inch of rain (0.24 inches), and Gainesville, Florida, only measured a tenth of an inch in April, its second driest April.

It was the third driest April in 132 years in South Carolina, and fifth driest in both North Carolina and Virginia, according to NOAA.

While some soaking rain arrived in other parts of the South before month's end, this persistent dry weather since winter left much of the South in severe to exceptional drought , and led to large wildfires in southern Georgia and Florida, among other areas.

( RECAP: Florida, Georgia Wildfires )

Las Vegas, Phoenix, Tucson, and Yuma, Arizona, didn't manage any measurable precipitation in April, though rainless Aprils have happened many times before in the Desert Southwest.

Summerlike April

Eight states from Missouri to Virginia had their warmest April in 132 years, according to NOAA's calculations.

Four other states — Arkansas, Maryland, North Carolina and Pennsylvania — had their second warmest April.

According to the Southeast Regional Climate Center, at least 19 major reporting stations with data since at least the 1960s either tied or set their warmest April in history, denoted by the red dots in the map below.

Among those major cities were Atlanta, Columbus, Indianapolis, Louisville, Nashville and Pittsburgh.

Over two dozen more cities had either their second or third warmest April, including Charlotte, Cincinnati, Phoenix, St. Louis and Raleigh.

Several eastern cities had a record number of April days with highs at least in the 80s , including Charlotte (17 days), New York City (5 days) and Washington (11 days), according to the National Weather Service.

The same persistent pattern that led to the dramatic contrast in precipitation in April also led to a lack of severe weather in parts of the Southeast where April has been a notorious month, particularly recently.

For the first April in 14 years, there was not a single tornado recorded in Alabama in April 2026. The state had 12 other tornado-less Aprils since 1950, prior to this year. Alabama averaged 16 tornadoes each April so far this century, according to NOAA's Storm Prediction Center.

There wasn't even a single tornado warning issued in the state for the first April since 2004 .

Severe weather was even more absent in neighboring Georgia. Only three reports of severe thunderstorms — all on April 3 — were tallied in the state all month, its quietest April for severe weather in 25 years.

The Plains and Midwest absorbed what the Southeast didn't experience.

Wisconsin — 27 confirmed tornadoes — tied its second most of any month since 1950, the only time April has been counted among the state's most tornadic months, according to the state climate office. Over 300 severe, tornado and flash flood warnings were issued in the state in April , the most of any single month on record.

All previous Badger State monthly tornado and "severe weather warnings issued" records were in a summer month prior to April 2026.

Jonathan Erdman is a senior meteorologist at weather.com and has been covering national and international weather since 1996. Extreme and bizarre weather are his favorite topics. Reach out to him on Bluesky , X (formerly Twitter) and Facebook .

id=\"wxn0\" />\u003Cp>The Eastern Pacific hurricane season begins Friday \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/climate/news/2026-05-07-super-el-nino-forecast-may2026\" rel=\"\">and an increasingly likely super El Niño\u003C/a> could turbocharge the 2026 season with at least some impacts possible in Mexico, Hawaii and the Southwest United States.\u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Why It Starts Earlier\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>But doesn't hurricane season start in June?\u003C/p>\u003Cp>That's true in the Atlantic Basin.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>The Eastern Pacific side — generally from Mexico's Pacific coast into the Central Pacific Ocean — starts on May 15.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>That's because conditions in the Eastern Pacific are typically favorable for development earlier than the Atlantic side, as you can see in the graphs from the National Hurricane Center below.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>The Eastern Pacific basin doesn't have to contend with \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/science/weather-explainers/news/2025-05-19-weather-words-saharan-air-layer-2025\" rel=\"\">surges of dry, dust-laden air from the Sahara Desert\u003C/a> that helps keep a lid on early development on the Atlantic side.\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn1\" />\u003Ch2>Where They Usually Go\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>You don't often hear as much about Eastern Pacific hurricanes.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>The large majority of them form somewhere off Mexico's Pacific coast, then move west or northwest over the open ocean and eventually fizzle without striking land.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>But as the map of hurricane tracks shows below, occasionally some hurricanes curl north or northeastward toward the Baja Peninsula, along the Gulf of California coast or Mexico's Pacific coast.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>While it's rare for tropical storms to remain intact as far north as the Southwest U.S., it's much more common for their remnant moisture or spin to boost summer monsoon thunderstorms with flooding rain in Arizona, California, Nevada, New Mexico and Utah.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Occasionally, some of those Eastern Pacific hurricanes fight off drier air and wind shear to make it as far west as Hawaii. Other storms can form closer to Hawaii and become a threat there.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>(\u003Cstrong>MORE: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2025-09-04-hawaii-hurricanes-tropical-storm-history-climatology\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Hawaii Hurricane History\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>)\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn2\" />\u003Ch2>Strong El Niño Impact\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>The central and eastern equatorial Pacific waters continue a steady march toward El Niño, a periodic warming of water in this region that can affect global weather patterns for months. This upcoming one could be a \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/climate/news/2026-05-07-super-el-nino-forecast-may2026\" rel=\"\">super El Niño, possibly record strong\u003C/a>.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>And this could impact the hurricane season.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>We examined data on storms, hurricanes and major hurricanes — Category 3 or stronger — in the Central and Eastern Pacific Basin from 1971 through 2025 compiled by Colorado State University tropical scientist Phil Klotzbach.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>From 1991 through 2020, an average hurricane season in the Central and Eastern Pacific generated between 16 and 17 storms, eight to nine of which were hurricanes and four to five of which reached at least Category 3 status. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>In the five years in which a strong El Niño developed sometime in the hurricane season, those tallies were boosted by an average of about four more storms, two to three more hurricanes and one to two more major hurricanes. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Unlike \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/science/weather-explainers/news/2026-03-23-el-nino-atlantic-basin-strong-hurricanes-tropical-storm\" rel=\"\">its squashing effect on the Atlantic Basin\u003C/a>, a stronger El Niño tends to produce more rising air and less hostile wind shear in the Central and Eastern Pacific basins, making the environment more favorable for tropical storms and hurricanes.\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn3\" />\u003Ch2>Strong El Niño Examples\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>The map below shows tracks of the 56 hurricanes that developed in the Central and Eastern Pacific basins during those five strong El Niño seasons since 1971.\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn4\" />\u003Cp>Many of those stayed out over the open ocean, as we previously alluded to, but there have been several notables:\u003C/p>\u003Cp>- In October 2015, \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/news/2024-10-22-on-todays-date-patricia-hurricane-record\" rel=\"\">Hurricane Patricia\u003C/a> became the most intense on record anywhere in the Western Hemisphere, peaking at 215 mph maximum sustained winds just off Mexico's Pacific coast before it slammed a sparsely populated area north of Manzanillo, Mexico.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>- In September 1997, the former Hurricane Nora survived as a tropical storm into the Lower Colorado River Valley. Up to 5.5 inches of rain in Southern California triggered street flooding in Palm Springs and San Diego. Winds knocked out power to 125,000 customers and up to 20-foot waves produced tidal flooding at Seal Beach.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>- In November 1982, Hurricane Iwa nailed Kauai, Hawaii, with destructive high winds and storm surge. Over $300 million in damage was reported in Kauai and Niihau and 2,300 homes were damaged. Iwa was Hawaii's costliest natural disaster at the time before a much stronger Hurricane Iniki raked Kauai 10 years later.\u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Wild Card: Ocean Heat Wave\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>As if a potentially record-strong El Niño wasn't enough to put a charge into the 2026 Central and Eastern Pacific hurricane season, there's another factor in play this year.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Ocean water is generally warm enough to support tropical storms and hurricanes from around Puerto Vallarta, Mexico, southward.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>But right now, there's a large blob of much warmer than average water in the Eastern Pacific hurricane zone extending as far north as off the U.S. West Coast and as far west as Hawaii and tropical areas near the International Date Line.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>According to an analysis by meteorologist Alex Boreham, parts of that zone are \u003Ca href=\"https://cyclonicwx.com/data/sst/oisst_sstrec_npac.png\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">record-warm for this time of year\u003C/a>.\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn5\" />\u003Cp>That doesn't mean we'll have tropical storms and hurricanes as far north as California this season. They're \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2023-08-17-california-tropical-storm-hurricane-history-hilary\" rel=\"\">extremely rare in California\u003C/a>. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>But this Pacific Ocean heat wave could give an additional boost to any Central and Eastern Pacific hurricanes this season. It could also allow them to last a bit longer when and if they are pulled northward toward Baja California or the Desert Southwest.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>(\u003Cstrong>TROPICAL HISTORY: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2023-08-17-california-tropical-storm-hurricane-history-hilary\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>California\u003C/strong>\u003C/a> | \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2025-09-04-hawaii-hurricanes-tropical-storm-history-climatology\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Hawaii\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>)\u003C/p>\u003Cp>So, while the \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/news/2026-04-16-weather-company-atmospheric-g2-hurricane-season-outlook\" rel=\"\">Atlantic hurricane season could be quieter\u003C/a> than usual, the opposite appears likely in the Pacific in 2026.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>(\u003Cstrong>ATLANTIC\u003C/strong> \u003Cstrong>IN DEPTH: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/science/weather-explainers/news/2026-03-23-el-nino-atlantic-basin-strong-hurricanes-tropical-storm\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Strong El Niño Hurricane Seasons\u003C/strong>\u003C/a> | \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2026-04-10-super-el-nino-hurricane-track-history\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Effects On Tracks\u003C/strong>\u003C/a> | \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/travel/news/2026-04-29-caribbean-travel-hurricane-season-el-nino\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Caribbean Travel Impacts\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>)\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn6\" />\u003Cp>\u003Cem>Jonathan Erdman is a senior meteorologist at weather.com and has been covering national and international weather since 1996. Extreme and bizarre weather are his favorite topics. id=\"wxn0\" />\u003Cp>The Eastern Pacific hurricane season begins Friday \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/climate/news/2026-05-07-super-el-nino-forecast-may2026\" rel=\"\">and an increasingly likely super El Niño\u003C/a> could turbocharge the 2026 season with at least some impacts possible in Mexico, Hawaii and the Southwest United States.\u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Why It Starts Earlier\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>But doesn't hurricane season start in June?\u003C/p>\u003Cp>That's true in the Atlantic Basin.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>The Eastern Pacific side — generally from Mexico's Pacific coast into the Central Pacific Ocean — starts on May 15.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>That's because conditions in the Eastern Pacific are typically favorable for development earlier than the Atlantic side, as you can see in the graphs from the National Hurricane Center below.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>The Eastern Pacific basin doesn't have to contend with \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/science/weather-explainers/news/2025-05-19-weather-words-saharan-air-layer-2025\" rel=\"\">surges of dry, dust-laden air from the Sahara Desert\u003C/a> that helps keep a lid on early development on the Atlantic side.\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn1\" />\u003Ch2>Where They Usually Go\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>You don't often hear as much about Eastern Pacific hurricanes.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>The large majority of them form somewhere off Mexico's Pacific coast, then move west or northwest over the open ocean and eventually fizzle without striking land.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>But as the map of hurricane tracks shows below, occasionally some hurricanes curl north or northeastward toward the Baja Peninsula, along the Gulf of California coast or Mexico's Pacific coast.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>While it's rare for tropical storms to remain intact as far north as the Southwest U.S., it's much more common for their remnant moisture or spin to boost summer monsoon thunderstorms with flooding rain in Arizona, California, Nevada, New Mexico and Utah.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Occasionally, some of those Eastern Pacific hurricanes fight off drier air and wind shear to make it as far west as Hawaii. Other storms can form closer to Hawaii and become a threat there.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>(\u003Cstrong>MORE: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2025-09-04-hawaii-hurricanes-tropical-storm-history-climatology\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Hawaii Hurricane History\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>)\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn2\" />\u003Ch2>Strong El Niño Impact\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>The central and eastern equatorial Pacific waters continue a steady march toward El Niño, a periodic warming of water in this region that can affect global weather patterns for months. This upcoming one could be a \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/climate/news/2026-05-07-super-el-nino-forecast-may2026\" rel=\"\">super El Niño, possibly record strong\u003C/a>.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>And this could impact the hurricane season.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>We examined data on storms, hurricanes and major hurricanes — Category 3 or stronger — in the Central and Eastern Pacific Basin from 1971 through 2025 compiled by Colorado State University tropical scientist Phil Klotzbach.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>From 1991 through 2020, an average hurricane season in the Central and Eastern Pacific generated between 16 and 17 storms, eight to nine of which were hurricanes and four to five of which reached at least Category 3 status. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>In the five years in which a strong El Niño developed sometime in the hurricane season, those tallies were boosted by an average of about four more storms, two to three more hurricanes and one to two more major hurricanes. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Unlike \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/science/weather-explainers/news/2026-03-23-el-nino-atlantic-basin-strong-hurricanes-tropical-storm\" rel=\"\">its squashing effect on the Atlantic Basin\u003C/a>, a stronger El Niño tends to produce more rising air and less hostile wind shear in the Central and Eastern Pacific basins, making the environment more favorable for tropical storms and hurricanes.\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn3\" />\u003Ch2>Strong El Niño Examples\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>The map below shows tracks of the 56 hurricanes that developed in the Central and Eastern Pacific basins during those five strong El Niño seasons since 1971.\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn4\" />\u003Cp>Many of those stayed out over the open ocean, as we previously alluded to, but there have been several notables:\u003C/p>\u003Cp>- In October 2015, \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/news/2024-10-22-on-todays-date-patricia-hurricane-record\" rel=\"\">Hurricane Patricia\u003C/a> became the most intense on record anywhere in the Western Hemisphere, peaking at 215 mph maximum sustained winds just off Mexico's Pacific coast before it slammed a sparsely populated area north of Manzanillo, Mexico.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>- In September 1997, the former Hurricane Nora survived as a tropical storm into the Lower Colorado River Valley. Up to 5.5 inches of rain in Southern California triggered street flooding in Palm Springs and San Diego. Winds knocked out power to 125,000 customers and up to 20-foot waves produced tidal flooding at Seal Beach.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>- In November 1982, Hurricane Iwa nailed Kauai, Hawaii, with destructive high winds and storm surge. Over $300 million in damage was reported in Kauai and Niihau and 2,300 homes were damaged. Iwa was Hawaii's costliest natural disaster at the time before a much stronger Hurricane Iniki raked Kauai 10 years later.\u003C/p>\u003Ch2>Wild Card: Ocean Heat Wave\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>As if a potentially record-strong El Niño wasn't enough to put a charge into the 2026 Central and Eastern Pacific hurricane season, there's another factor in play this year.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Ocean water is generally warm enough to support tropical storms and hurricanes from around Puerto Vallarta, Mexico, southward.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>But right now, there's a large blob of much warmer than average water in the Eastern Pacific hurricane zone extending as far north as off the U.S. West Coast and as far west as Hawaii and tropical areas near the International Date Line.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>According to an analysis by meteorologist Alex Boreham, parts of that zone are \u003Ca href=\"https://cyclonicwx.com/data/sst/oisst_sstrec_npac.png\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"\">record-warm for this time of year\u003C/a>.\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn5\" />\u003Cp>That doesn't mean we'll have tropical storms and hurricanes as far north as California this season. They're \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2023-08-17-california-tropical-storm-hurricane-history-hilary\" rel=\"\">extremely rare in California\u003C/a>. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>But this Pacific Ocean heat wave could give an additional boost to any Central and Eastern Pacific hurricanes this season. It could also allow them to last a bit longer when and if they are pulled northward toward Baja California or the Desert Southwest.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>(\u003Cstrong>TROPICAL HISTORY: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2023-08-17-california-tropical-storm-hurricane-history-hilary\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>California\u003C/strong>\u003C/a> | \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2025-09-04-hawaii-hurricanes-tropical-storm-history-climatology\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Hawaii\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>)\u003C/p>\u003Cp>So, while the \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/news/2026-04-16-weather-company-atmospheric-g2-hurricane-season-outlook\" rel=\"\">Atlantic hurricane season could be quieter\u003C/a> than usual, the opposite appears likely in the Pacific in 2026.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>(\u003Cstrong>ATLANTIC\u003C/strong> \u003Cstrong>IN DEPTH: \u003C/strong>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/science/weather-explainers/news/2026-03-23-el-nino-atlantic-basin-strong-hurricanes-tropical-storm\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Strong El Niño Hurricane Seasons\u003C/strong>\u003C/a> | \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2026-04-10-super-el-nino-hurricane-track-history\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Effects On Tracks\u003C/strong>\u003C/a> | \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/travel/news/2026-04-29-caribbean-travel-hurricane-season-el-nino\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Caribbean Travel Impacts\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>)\u003C/p>\u003Cdiv id=\"wxn6\" />\u003Cp>\u003Cem>Jonathan Erdman is a senior meteorologist at weather.com and has been covering national and international weather since 1996. gold_text:
The strong El Niño ahead could turbocharge the Eastern Pacific hurricane season. Another wild card may provide an additional boost. Here's what it could mean, including for parts of the U.S.

The Eastern Pacific hurricane season begins Friday and an increasingly likely super El Niño could turbocharge the 2026 season with at least some impacts possible in Mexico, Hawaii and the Southwest United States.

Why It Starts Earlier

But doesn't hurricane season start in June?

That's true in the Atlantic Basin.

The Eastern Pacific side — generally from Mexico's Pacific coast into the Central Pacific Ocean — starts on May 15.

That's because conditions in the Eastern Pacific are typically favorable for development earlier than the Atlantic side, as you can see in the graphs from the National Hurricane Center below.

The Eastern Pacific basin doesn't have to contend with surges of dry, dust-laden air from the Sahara Desert that helps keep a lid on early development on the Atlantic side.

Where They Usually Go

You don't often hear as much about Eastern Pacific hurricanes.

The large majority of them form somewhere off Mexico's Pacific coast, then move west or northwest over the open ocean and eventually fizzle without striking land.

But as the map of hurricane tracks shows below, occasionally some hurricanes curl north or northeastward toward the Baja Peninsula, along the Gulf of California coast or Mexico's Pacific coast.

While it's rare for tropical storms to remain intact as far north as the Southwest U.S., it's much more common for their remnant moisture or spin to boost summer monsoon thunderstorms with flooding rain in Arizona, California, Nevada, New Mexico and Utah.

Occasionally, some of those Eastern Pacific hurricanes fight off drier air and wind shear to make it as far west as Hawaii. Other storms can form closer to Hawaii and become a threat there.

( MORE: Hawaii Hurricane History )

Strong El Niño Impact

The central and eastern equatorial Pacific waters continue a steady march toward El Niño, a periodic warming of water in this region that can affect global weather patterns for months. This upcoming one could be a super El Niño, possibly record strong .

And this could impact the hurricane season.

We examined data on storms, hurricanes and major hurricanes — Category 3 or stronger — in the Central and Eastern Pacific Basin from 1971 through 2025 compiled by Colorado State University tropical scientist Phil Klotzbach.

From 1991 through 2020, an average hurricane season in the Central and Eastern Pacific generated between 16 and 17 storms, eight to nine of which were hurricanes and four to five of which reached at least Category 3 status.

In the five years in which a strong El Niño developed sometime in the hurricane season, those tallies were boosted by an average of about four more storms, two to three more hurricanes and one to two more major hurricanes.

Unlike its squashing effect on the Atlantic Basin , a stronger El Niño tends to produce more rising air and less hostile wind shear in the Central and Eastern Pacific basins, making the environment more favorable for tropical storms and hurricanes.

Strong El Niño Examples

The map below shows tracks of the 56 hurricanes that developed in the Central and Eastern Pacific basins during those five strong El Niño seasons since 1971.

Many of those stayed out over the open ocean, as we previously alluded to, but there have been several notables:

- In October 2015, Hurricane Patricia became the most intense on record anywhere in the Western Hemisphere, peaking at 215 mph maximum sustained winds just off Mexico's Pacific coast before it slammed a sparsely populated area north of Manzanillo, Mexico.

- In September 1997, the former Hurricane Nora survived as a tropical storm into the Lower Colorado River Valley. Up to 5.5 inches of rain in Southern California triggered street flooding in Palm Springs and San Diego. Winds knocked out power to 125,000 customers and up to 20-foot waves produced tidal flooding at Seal Beach.

- In November 1982, Hurricane Iwa nailed Kauai, Hawaii, with destructive high winds and storm surge. Over $300 million in damage was reported in Kauai and Niihau and 2,300 homes were damaged. Iwa was Hawaii's costliest natural disaster at the time before a much stronger Hurricane Iniki raked Kauai 10 years later.

Wild Card: Ocean Heat Wave

As if a potentially record-strong El Niño wasn't enough to put a charge into the 2026 Central and Eastern Pacific hurricane season, there's another factor in play this year.

Ocean water is generally warm enough to support tropical storms and hurricanes from around Puerto Vallarta, Mexico, southward.

But right now, there's a large blob of much warmer than average water in the Eastern Pacific hurricane zone extending as far north as off the U.S. West Coast and as far west as Hawaii and tropical areas near the International Date Line.

According to an analysis by meteorologist Alex Boreham, parts of that zone are record-warm for this time of year .

That doesn't mean we'll have tropical storms and hurricanes as far north as California this season. They're extremely rare in California .

But this Pacific Ocean heat wave could give an additional boost to any Central and Eastern Pacific hurricanes this season. It could also allow them to last a bit longer when and if they are pulled northward toward Baja California or the Desert Southwest.

( TROPICAL HISTORY: California | Hawaii )

So, while the Atlantic hurricane season could be quieter than usual, the opposite appears likely in the Pacific in 2026.

( ATLANTIC IN DEPTH: Strong El Niño Hurricane Seasons | Effects On Tracks | Caribbean Travel Impacts )

Jonathan Erdman is a senior meteorologist at weather.com and has been covering national and international weather since 1996. Extreme and bizarre weather are his favorite topics. Reach out to him on Bluesky , X (formerly Twitter) and Facebook .

id=\"wxn0\" />\u003Cp>\u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/forums#/category/extreme-and-hazardous-weather/hurricanes/hurricane-season-2026-and-hurricane-preparedness-ask-me-anything_0670dc\" rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Click Here To Join The Discussion Right Now\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>\u003Cstrong>!\u003C/strong>\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Hurricane season is creeping up on us, and now is the perfect time get all your family, plans and hurricane kits together. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>What questions do you have? Join me now to have all of them answered. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>As a reminder, \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/news/2026-04-16-weather-company-atmospheric-g2-hurricane-season-outlook\" rel=\"\">here's our outlook for this upcoming hurricane season.\u003C/a> \u003C/p>\u003Cp>I have been a Senior Digital Meteorologist with \u003Ca href=\"weather.com\" rel=\"\">weather.com\u003C/a> for nearly 10 years. My favorite weather is tropical weather, but I also enjoy covering high-impact weather and news stories and winter storms. I am a two-time graduate of Florida State University and a proud graduate of St. Petersburg College. \u003Ca href=\"https://x.com/JonathanBelles\" rel=\"\">You can follow me here.\u003C/a>\u003C/p>\u003Ch2>What You Should Know\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>Registration is required to participate in our live AMA sessions, ask questions, and engage with others. This registration will also allow you to comment on any of our articles or videos. Just scroll down to the bottom of any article to join the conversation. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>We won’t be answering questions about your specific town's hurricane landfall forecast since it is much too early to know what cities could be hit this season, although we could lend \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2026-04-10-super-el-nino-hurricane-track-history\" rel=\"\">a few guesses to where would hurricanes won't go\u003C/a>.  \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Here at The Weather Channel, we'll be hosting Ask Me Anything sessions on various topics. These sessions are meant to connect you directly to our experts to ask just about anything that might be driving you nuts or to answer your weather curiosities. We'll be using our brand new Forums feature to drive conversation and we invite you to jump in, too. If you see something you have some insight on, please hop into a thread.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>If the volume of questions is high, we may have a second meteorologist join in. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>\u003Cem>Hint: You can submit your questions and photos now. rel=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Click Here To Join The Discussion Right Now\u003C/strong>\u003C/a>\u003Cstrong>!\u003C/strong>\u003C/p>\u003Cp>Hurricane season is creeping up on us, and now is the perfect time Join me now to have all of them answered. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>As a reminder, \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/news/weather/news/2026-04-16-weather-company-atmospheric-g2-hurricane-season-outlook\" rel=\"\">here's our outlook for this upcoming hurricane season.\u003C/a> \u003C/p>\u003Cp>I have been a Senior Digital Meteorologist with \u003Ca href=\"weather.com\" rel=\"\">weather.com\u003C/a> for nearly 10 years. My favorite weather is tropical weather, but I also enjoy covering high-impact weather and news stories and winter storms. I am a two-time graduate of Florida State University and a proud graduate of St. Petersburg College. \u003Ca href=\"https://x.com/JonathanBelles\" rel=\"\">You can follow me here.\u003C/a>\u003C/p>\u003Ch2>What You Should Know\u003C/h2>\u003Cp>Registration is required to participate in our live AMA sessions, ask questions, and engage with others. This registration will also allow you to comment on any of our articles or videos. Just scroll down to the bottom of any article to join the conversation. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>We won’t be answering questions about your specific town's hurricane landfall forecast since it is much too early to know what cities could be hit this season, although we could lend \u003Ca href=\"https://weather.com/storms/hurricane/news/2026-04-10-super-el-nino-hurricane-track-history\" rel=\"\">a few guesses to where would hurricanes won't go\u003C/a>.  \u003C/p>\u003Cp>Here at The Weather Channel, we'll be hosting Ask Me Anything sessions on various topics. These sessions are meant to connect you directly to our experts to ask just about anything that might be driving you nuts or to answer your weather curiosities. We'll be using our brand new Forums feature to drive conversation and we invite you to jump in, too. If you see something you have some insight on, please hop into a thread.\u003C/p>\u003Cp>If the volume of questions is high, we may have a second meteorologist join in. \u003C/p>\u003Cp>\u003Cem>Hint: You can submit your questions and photos now. gold_text:
Join Senior Digital Meteorologist Jonathan Belles right now to get all your questions answered.

Click Here To Join The Discussion Right Now !

Hurricane season is creeping up on us, and now is the perfect time get all your family, plans and hurricane kits together.

What questions do you have? Join me now to have all of them answered.

As a reminder, here's our outlook for this upcoming hurricane season.

I have been a Senior Digital Meteorologist with weather.com for nearly 10 years. My favorite weather is tropical weather, but I also enjoy covering high-impact weather and news stories and winter storms. I am a two-time graduate of Florida State University and a proud graduate of St. Petersburg College. You can follow me here.

What You Should Know

Registration is required to participate in our live AMA sessions, ask questions, and engage with others. This registration will also allow you to comment on any of our articles or videos. Just scroll down to the bottom of any article to join the conversation.

We won’t be answering questions about your specific town's hurricane landfall forecast since it is much too early to know what cities could be hit this season, although we could lend a few guesses to where would hurricanes won't go .

Here at The Weather Channel, we'll be hosting Ask Me Anything sessions on various topics. These sessions are meant to connect you directly to our experts to ask just about anything that might be driving you nuts or to answer your weather curiosities. We'll be using our brand new Forums feature to drive conversation and we invite you to jump in, too. If you see something you have some insight on, please hop into a thread.

If the volume of questions is high, we may have a second meteorologist join in.

Hint: You can submit your questions and photos now. Let's get it started!
